question.text: How is income of AIF Category III taxed, and how does this differ from Category I and II?

answer.text: Category III has pass-through status; income is taxable only for unit-holders
All three categories have pass-through status; income is taxable only for unit-holders
Category III income is exempt; Category I and II income is taxable at the fund level
Category III has NO pass-through status; income is taxable at the fund level at maximum marginal rate. Category I and II enjoy pass-through status.

explanation.text: **The Logic:** AIF Category I and II enjoy pass-through status -- income (other than business income) is exempt at the fund level and taxable in the hands of unit-holders. Category III does NOT have pass-through status -- income is taxed at the fund level at the maximum marginal rate (highest slab rate applicable). This is the most significant tax distinction across AIF categories. **The Trap:** "All three categories have pass-through" is tempting because pass-through is generally considered a benefit of the AIF structure. However, Category III was deliberately excluded from pass-through to prevent tax arbitrage through hedge fund strategies. **Cross-Exam Context:** NISM Series X-B heavily tests this distinction as it directly impacts after-tax returns for investors and is crucial for investment suitability assessment. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** The maximum marginal rate taxation on Category III AIFs means returns are significantly reduced by taxes at the fund level before distribution. Always present Category III returns on a post-tax basis to clients for accurate comparison with other investments.
